meta.title: Realism scorecard element catalog
meta.note: Canonical per-element checklist. Regenerate scorecard.html via code/build_scorecard.py after editing.

elements.label: Multi-segment graph with enforced path behavior
elements.desc: The network is split into zones (such as DMZ, user, server, admin, and OT), with access rules (ACLs) applied per segment and per protocol. Reachability follows that routing and policy rather than every host reaching every other, so scans, sessions, and blocked or denied connections reflect the real layout. When the defender isolates a host or changes a policy, the set of reachable paths visibly changes.

elements.label: Perimeter and egress asymmetry
elements.desc: The network's perimeter is not uniform: some services are reachable from outside the organization while others are internal only. Outbound traffic is controlled separately from inbound (for example by web proxies and DNS filtering), and the different routes out of the network are not equivalent, each carrying a different chance of detection.

elements.label: Network devices as manageable entities
elements.desc: Network devices such as routers, switches, and firewalls are manageable entities, with management surfaces such as a CLI, SNMP, and configuration files, and their configuration-change events are observable. This supports network-device persistence and traffic-signaling techniques.

elements.label: Layer 2/3 protocol fidelity
elements.desc: Layer 2 and 3 protocols such as ARP, DHCP, LLMNR/NBT-NS, SMB signing, and 802.1X behave realistically, including their real weaknesses (poisoning, relay, NTLM capture) and the countermeasures against them.

elements.label: Position-dependent traffic visibility
elements.desc: What an attacker can sniff or intercept depends on where it sits in the network, and port mirroring (SPAN) is possible only where the topology supports it. The defender's visibility likewise depends on sensor placement and which paths are monitored, not on global access.

elements.label: Lateral reachability and file transfer
elements.desc: Lateral-movement and file-transfer mechanisms such as SMB admin shares, SCP/SFTP, WinRM, and RDP drive mapping work only where the topology allows, and the transfers they perform generate realistic internal network flows.

elements.label: Discoverable (not given) topology
elements.desc: The topology is discovered, not handed to the agent: scans return partial, segmentation-dependent, and noisy results, so the agent must build its map of the network through discovery.

elements.label: Staging and exfil-path asymmetry
elements.desc: Hosts differ in their external connectivity, which forces realistic staging decisions and supports moving data from internal hosts toward the network edge.

elements.label: Remote-access and cross-organizational boundaries
elements.desc: Remote-access and cross-organizational entry points such as VPN concentrators, jump hosts, vendor or MSP access, and federated reachability are modeled as boundary-crossing mechanisms, each with its own authentication and logging.

elements.label: Believable decoy placement
elements.desc: Decoys sit in plausible network locations, segments, routes, and exposure patterns, rather than in obviously isolated trap zones.

elements.label: Real, versioned, fingerprintable daemons
elements.desc: Real services (for example SSH, RDP, SMB, HTTP(S), LDAP, databases, mail, WinRM, DCOM) listen and respond in a protocol-conformant way, with realistic banners and version-specific quirks. Each service makes its own access decisions from the requester's identity, session, the applicable policy, and the resource requested, rather than a single global allow or deny.

elements.label: Exploitable vulnerable builds with heterogeneous patch state
elements.desc: Hosts run specific vulnerable software versions with real, version-specific vulnerabilities (such as EternalBlue, BlueKeep, Log4Shell, PrintNightmare, Zerologon) that take real exploit parameters. Patching is uneven across the network, so some machines are fully up to date while others still run older, exploitable builds.

elements.label: Web applications with real code paths and session stack
elements.desc: Web applications have real code paths and a real session stack, with exploitable vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, SSRF, remote code execution, and deserialization, realistic directory structures, and cookies carrying HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite attributes. Federated flows such as OAuth, OIDC, and SAML issue tokens that become forgeable if signing keys are compromised, and session validity, token expiry, and MFA are enforced on high-risk flows.

elements.label: Email infrastructure
elements.desc: Email infrastructure (for example SMTP, IMAP, Exchange, Graph) works end to end, with real mailboxes, enforceable SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, and mail-rule creation available as an abusable primitive.

elements.label: DNS infrastructure
elements.desc: Authoritative DNS serves a full record set with realistic TTL and NXDOMAIN behavior, its outbound queries are observable, and it is faithful enough to support fast-flux and domain-generation-algorithm (DGA) analysis.

elements.label: File shares and enterprise repositories
elements.desc: Enterprise file stores behave realistically, for example SMB, NFS, or DFS shares with real ACLs and content; Confluence, SharePoint, or wikis with plausible organizational content; and code repositories that contain accidentally-committed secrets. Shares and document libraries check role- and resource-based permissions before returning content.

elements.label: Authentication-service and PKI surfaces
elements.desc: Authentication services validate credentials realistically: password attempts are checked as real ones would be under password spraying and brute force, with lockout, throttling, and MFA applied per policy, and captured password hashes appear in realistic formats. A certificate authority (such as ADCS) is reachable as an enrollment and authentication service.

elements.label: Network-device management services
elements.desc: Network-device management surfaces are present, such as SNMP with guessable community strings, a device CLI, and TFTP or FTP configuration backups that contain real architectural data.

elements.label: Enterprise deployment and supply-chain services
elements.desc: Enterprise deployment and supply-chain services are modeled, such as SCCM/MECM and Ansible with agents on endpoints, package managers and registries, CI/CD pipelines that produce signed artifacts, and update mechanisms with explicit trust relationships.

elements.label: Legitimate RMM and dual-use web services
elements.desc: Legitimate remote-management tools such as TeamViewer, AnyDesk, and ScreenConnect are installable and functional, and cloud storage, code repositories, and messaging APIs are reachable as potential covert command-and-control channels. Their legitimacy is what defeats naive detection.

elements.label: Revocation-aware service state
elements.desc: Services, token consumers, session caches, and application connections react to credential revocation and restoration according to their own session and cache semantics, so a revoked credential takes effect as it would in reality.

elements.label: Operational recovery surfaces
elements.desc: Backup, restore, rollback, and recovery exist as real operational surfaces, with realistic targets, dependencies, permissions, and workflow steps.

elements.label: Dependency-aware response effects
elements.desc: Service dependencies react meaningfully: when a hardening, isolation, eviction, or restore action affects a required identity, network, storage, database, or application dependency, the dependent services respond accordingly.

elements.label: Believable decoy service context
elements.desc: Decoy services present plausible protocols, data, workflows, and dependencies for the business context they imitate, and behave consistently with the real services around them.

elements.label: Persistence surface
elements.desc: The host provides realistic persistence mechanisms, and where present they behave to specification, survive reboot, and emit telemetry when modified; they also double as host-state objects a defender can inspect, harden, and restore. These mechanisms span Windows (Run keys, services, Task Scheduler, WMI subscriptions), Linux (systemd, cron, init.d, rc, udev), and macOS (launchd, LoginItems, emond).

elements.label: Execution-flow control and scripting engines
elements.desc: The host supports realistic execution-flow abuse and scripting, such as loader and module-resolution hijacking (DLL search order, LD_PRELOAD, dyld, COM), signed-binary proxy execution, living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBins), and scripting engines that emit engine-level telemetry. It also offers OS-native enforcement points such as application allowlisting, syscall filtering, and a local firewall.

elements.label: Process, memory, and privilege-boundary primitives
elements.desc: The host exposes real process- and memory-level primitives such as process injection, token manipulation and impersonation, reflective and in-memory loading, and IPC endpoints (named pipes, DCOM, D-Bus, XPC), together with genuine privilege-elevation boundaries (UAC, sudo/setuid, TCC, polkit) that have realistic bypass paths. Effects are not permanent by fiat: killing a payload may be undone by a service manager or a scheduled task, and terminating a protected process requires sufficient authority and can fail.

elements.label: Configuration stores, filesystem semantics, and kernel/trust layer
elements.desc: OS-native configuration surfaces such as the registry, /etc, and plists carry real ACLs and auditing. Filesystem features behave realistically, for example NTFS alternate data streams, reparse points, Unix modes, extended attributes, the immutable flag, direct-volume access, and timestamp tampering. The kernel and trust layer are exposed and verifiable, including the signed-driver path, bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver (BYOVD), rootkit hooks, code-signing trust, and boot and platform trust (Secure Boot, TPM).

elements.label: Credential material exposure on the endpoint
elements.desc: Credentials live where the real operating system puts them and can be harvested through realistic paths, such as LSASS, the SAM, DPAPI, cached logons, LSA secrets, and Kerberos tickets on Windows; /etc/shadow and the keyring on Linux; the Keychain on macOS; and browser stores, SSH keys, and cloud-CLI configuration files across platforms.

elements.label: Discovery, evasion, and anti-forensics surface
elements.desc: Host-inventory queries return consistent, realistically large results. Evasion checks and guardrails, such as virtual-machine, debugger, and time-based detection, return plausible answers, and anti-forensics primitives such as hidden files and users or log and history clearing work as they would on a real OS, leaving their own tell-tale artifacts.

elements.label: Impact primitives and document-execution behavior
elements.desc: Destructive actions such as file encryption, shadow-copy deletion, disk wipe, service stop, and reboot, and document-based execution paths such as Office macros, PDF JavaScript, and LNK or ISO containers, behave according to real OS semantics.

elements.label: Restorable host objects
elements.desc: Files, binaries, packages, disks, configuration, and system state exist as objects that can be compared against a known-good source and restored through a defender workflow, with verifiable integrity.

elements.label: Populated directory and local identity stores
elements.desc: The environment has a populated directory and local identity stores, such as an AD, LDAP, or FreeIPA directory with realistic users, groups, OUs, nested memberships, SPNs, and misconfigured ACLs, together with local stores (the SAM, /etc/passwd and shadow, and cached domain credentials) distributed unevenly across hosts.

elements.label: Credential landscape with realistic material in real locations
elements.desc: Credentials form a realistic landscape rather than a uniform set: passwords vary in strength, are reused, and include default and service-account credentials. Secrets also accumulate through actual use in the places attackers look, such as Group Policy Preferences cpassword, unattend.xml files, and shell history.

elements.label: Kerberos and NTLM protocol fidelity
elements.desc: Kerberos and NTLM behave faithfully: AS and TGS exchanges with encryption negotiation and attacks such as Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, S4U, and PKINIT; NTLMv1 and v2 challenge-response with relay to SMB, LDAP, HTTP, or ADCS; and legacy poisonable protocols such as LLMNR, NBT-NS, and mDNS.

elements.label: Federation, SSO, and web session material
elements.desc: Federation, single sign-on, and web session material are modeled: a SAML identity provider with signing keys, OAuth and OIDC flows with access and refresh tokens, JWTs carrying claims, and cross-tenant federation. Web session cookies and API tokens can be stolen to enable authenticated replay, and a single login establishes coherent identity-provider, directory, workstation, and service session state.

elements.label: MFA as a design space and auth-process modification surface
elements.desc: MFA is a genuine design space and an auth-process attack surface. Factor types vary (such as TOTP, SMS, push, FIDO2, smart-card), a modeled user is subject to push fatigue, and per-app or conditional-access enforcement has realistic gaps. Auth-package, SSP, PAM, and skeleton-key modification paths exist, and MFA outcomes are recorded with user and source context.

elements.label: Account lifecycle and tiered administration
elements.desc: Accounts have a realistic lifecycle and tiered administration: create, enable, disable, reset, and group-change operations that leave logged artifacts; separation of administrative tiers (Tier 0/1/2); local-administrator passwords that are either unique per machine (as LAPS provides) or the same across many hosts; and managed service accounts (MSA/gMSA) rather than service accounts with static plaintext passwords.

elements.label: Session reality and valid-account usage
elements.desc: Logon sessions behave realistically: logon types (interactive, network, RDP) are distinguishable, authenticated sessions leave cached tickets and hashes in LSASS, and runas, sudo, su, and S4U produce real events that enable token theft and session hijacking.

elements.label: Certificate and PKI identity
elements.desc: Certificate-based identity is modeled: a certificate authority (ADCS) with the well-known template misconfigurations (ESC1 through ESC15), client-certificate authentication that services accept, observable issuance, renewal, and revocation events, and code-signing and S/MIME certificates as distinct surfaces.

elements.label: Enumeration and identity-relationship surface
elements.desc: The identity system can be enumerated as it would be in reality. Queries through net user, Get-ADUser, LDAP, or BloodHound-style graph extraction return realistically large, internally consistent results covering users, groups, sessions, SPNs, ACLs, trusts, tiering relationships, and account state, all reflecting the populated directory.

elements.label: Human-layer impersonation and external-to-internal bridges
elements.desc: Human-layer impersonation is possible: email spoofing against domains that enforce SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, internal spearphishing from compromised mailboxes, and lookalike-domain impersonation. External-to-internal identities such as vendor or MSP accounts, B2B guests, and service principals exist as distinct populations with their own delegated rights.

elements.label: Policy-bound authorization as identity-layer state
elements.desc: Authorization is a first-class part of the identity layer, not a static host-local list. Access to admin shares, SaaS resources, or sensitive APIs is derived from group membership, token claims, role assignments, or conditional-access policy, and the meaning and lifecycle of that policy are themselves modeled state.

elements.label: Propagating credential control and access recovery
elements.desc: Credential and account state changes (revocation, invalidation, reissue, unlock, restoration) propagate with realistic scope, timing, and residual state. Operational recovery workflows exist as defender actions, for example reviewing an account, then unlocking it, reissuing credentials, restoring group membership, and validating the result.

elements.label: Believable deceptive identities
elements.desc: Decoy accounts, credentials, tokens, or personas fit the surrounding naming conventions, roles, privileges, and placement, with believable metadata and access scope.

elements.label: Real wall-clock progression
elements.desc: Time advances as real, fine-grained wall-clock time rather than in per-action turns. Events, files, and logs carry monotonic timestamps, uptime and reboot history are consistent, event history is preserved in order, and NTP or time-source manipulation is available as a modeled primitive.

elements.label: Scheduled execution and reboot semantics
elements.desc: Scheduled tasks (cron, Task Scheduler, systemd timers) fire on their configured schedule rather than on an agent step, and jitter can be expressed. Reboots actually happen and clear non-persistent footholds, and persistence, revocation, and restore state carry across reboot and reset boundaries to affect later actions.

elements.label: Session, token, and credential lifetimes
elements.desc: Sessions, tokens, and credentials expire on real elapsed time: Kerberos ticket-granting-ticket and renewal windows are enforced, and OAuth access and refresh tokens, cookies, certificates, and password-age and lockout counters all expire and reset accordingly.

elements.label: Human activity rhythms
elements.desc: Human activity has realistic rhythms: business hours are distinguishable from off-hours in authentication and traffic baselines, there is weekday, weekend, and holiday variation, and shift patterns create variability in how quickly activity is noticed.

elements.label: Long-horizon engagement support
elements.desc: Simulated time can span weeks or months, and dwell holds (a foothold at T0 persists to T+30 days) while logs accumulate and patch levels drift. This supports long-dwell objectives such as espionage, evasive red-teaming, and threat hunting.

elements.label: Event-correlation windows and rate-limiting
elements.desc: Timing-based defenses operate on real time: correlation windows apply to SIEM rolling-window rules, IDS/IPS signatures, and network-flow analysis, which all fire on actual timing over finite retention. Account lockout, API rate limits (HTTP 429s), and connection-rate caps are likewise enforced in real time, so brute force must pace itself within them.

elements.label: Delay-based evasion and staged operations
elements.desc: Delays have real effect. Sleep and NtDelayExecution actually suspend execution, time-based guardrails and long-delay detonation behave as intended, and staged operations such as multi-stage C2, scheduled transfers, and low-and-slow exfiltration run on real inter-stage delays.

elements.label: State decay and temporal detection signals
elements.desc: State ages on real timers: DNS, ARP, credential, and Kerberos caches expire on their TTLs, logs rotate, shadow copies age, and certificates approach expiry. Unusual timing then surfaces as anomalies, for example a 3 a.m. logon, a weekend transfer, a regular beaconing cadence, or a dormant account reactivating.

elements.label: Non-instant defender effects with observable before/after
elements.desc: Defender actions take effect over time rather than instantly: revocation, containment, eviction, and recovery unfold, so a credential revocation blocks new sessions immediately but cached or derived tokens only after they expire. Defenders can observe when a quarantine takes effect and whether a restored service stays stable.

elements.label: Retained containment and recovery history
elements.desc: Earlier response actions (isolation, unlock, restore, configuration rollback) stay visible during later investigation, and the timeline records when each response fired and what state it changed, supporting after-the-fact verification.

elements.label: Perimeter, egress, and traffic inspection
elements.desc: Perimeter and traffic-inspection controls are present, such as stateful firewalls, a web application firewall (WAF) or IPS, web proxies with TLS inspection, DNS filtering, and a cloud access security broker (a CASB, which mediates and monitors access to cloud services), along with rate limiting and connection-state caps on exposed services.

elements.label: Email security stack
elements.desc: Email is fronted by a realistic threat-detection stack, distinct from the underlying mail service: a gateway that sandboxes attachments and detonates URLs, mailbox auditing that alerts on forwarding-rule changes, and detection of anomalous internal sending.

elements.label: Endpoint protection, EDR, and exploit mitigations
elements.desc: Endpoints carry realistic protection and exploit mitigations, such as antivirus with AMSI and behavioral detection, usermode API hooks and kernel telemetry, tamper protection, memory mitigations (DEP, ASLR, CFG, CET) and kernel mitigations, Credential Guard, LSA Protection, and VBS, and container or sandbox policies.

elements.label: Application-execution controls
elements.desc: Application-execution controls are available, such as AppLocker, WDAC, SRP, Gatekeeper, or fapolicyd with tunable policy, script-engine controls (PowerShell ExecutionPolicy, Constrained Language Mode), Office macro policies, and SmartScreen or Gatekeeper prompts.

elements.label: Authentication hardening
elements.desc: Authentication can be hardened, such as account lockout, smart lockout, and CAPTCHA; MFA via conditional access with realistic bypass gaps; NTLM restriction, SMB signing, LDAP channel binding, and Kerberos-only enforcement; and federation hardening (token binding, step-up authentication, device compliance).

elements.label: Patching and configuration hardening
elements.desc: Patching and configuration hardening are modeled as ongoing, partial, and time-varying: per-host patch state is heterogeneous, with a permanent end-of-life subset, CIS-style baselines are applied unevenly, and legacy-protocol disablement is a per-host configuration.

elements.label: Segmentation enforcement
elements.desc: Beyond the static network layout described under topological realism, segmentation is operated as an active defensive control: firewalls, host-based firewalls, VLAN ACLs, and microsegmentation not only block but also log, east-west traffic is inspected, and zero-trust or identity-aware gateways enforce reachability by identity. This gives segmentation detection value and lets the defender tighten it in response.

elements.label: Detection rule library and cloud-plane controls
elements.desc: A detection rule library and cloud-plane controls exist, such as Sysmon, EDR, and SIEM signature and behavioral rules with tunable coverage gaps, UEBA baselines built over benign activity, IAM policies, SCPs, and RBAC that restrict API actions, and cloud posture tools (CSPM, GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud).

elements.label: Data-loss prevention controls
elements.desc: Data-loss-prevention controls inspect content across endpoint, email, web, and cloud channels, using heuristics such as volume anomalies, archive encryption, and sensitive-data classification, and can block, warn, quarantine, or alert on suspicious data movement.

elements.label: Response, deception, and control-configuration action space
elements.desc: Defensive controls, responses, and deceptions are imperfect and configurable, not perfect oracles. They produce false positives and negatives, can be evaded (for example via obfuscation, LOLBins, BYOVD, or AMSI bypass) or tampered with, and each defender's coverage is scoped. Defenders can deploy, tune, and push them, but changes take time to roll out and carry operational cost and a burden of verifying they worked.
elements.anchors: T1531, T1685, T1027, T1218, UC-D2, UC-D4, UC-D5; D3-DE, D3-DO, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED, D3-MFA, D3-PH

elements.label: Simulated users with role and behavioral diversity
elements.desc: Simulated users have realistic role and behavioral diversity: multiple personas (such as developer, HR, finance, executive, sysadmin, helpdesk) with distinct application profiles, working hours, and access patterns, and a realistic workforce mix (remote, mobile, shared machines).

elements.label: User interactive input and active sessions
elements.desc: Users generate real interactive input and hold live sessions: keyboard and mouse events a keylogger could capture, clipboard use for legitimate copy and paste, and screen content, active windows, and console, RDP, or SSH sessions that exist because someone actually used them and are therefore hijackable.

elements.label: Browsing, document authorship, and accumulated web state
elements.desc: Users browse real sites and authenticate to web apps, and web state accumulates over time: browser password stores, cookies, and autofill. Role-appropriate documents land where real work would put them, and most-recently-used lists and author metadata reflect genuine activity.

elements.label: Email activity
elements.desc: Email shows realistic usage: send and receive volumes, accumulated mailbox content, PST and OST files, calendar and distribution-list traffic, and external correspondents that provide a path for phishing in and out.

elements.label: Credential-use traffic and lived-in accumulation
elements.desc: Because users authenticate repeatedly, credential use is visible on the wire as Kerberos, NTLM, and SSO exchanges, and users type passwords into real prompts. The lived-in credential artifacts an attacker later harvests exist precisely because people have logged on over time, rather than being placed there for the scenario.

elements.label: Administrative and service baseline
elements.desc: Administrators and services generate a legitimate baseline that provides cover: sysadmins run tools such as PowerShell, WMI, PsExec, SSH, and Ansible for real reasons, and scheduled maintenance, patching, backups, updater check-ins, telemetry agents, and MDM or SCCM run on realistic cadences. As a result, living-off-the-land tool use has a legitimate baseline to hide within.

elements.label: Network and data-volume baselines
elements.desc: Network and data volumes have realistic baselines: east-west traffic from shares, mail, printing, and AD replication; outbound web, SaaS, update, and telemetry traffic; a realistic DNS distribution that supports anomaly detection; and per-user and per-destination upload-volume baselines.

elements.label: Share, repository, and peripheral activity
elements.desc: Shared drives and collaboration sites (Confluence, SharePoint, wikis) are accessed in ways that exercise ACLs and generate access logs, repositories are read and written, and peripheral events (USB, printer, Bluetooth) happen occasionally, all of which provides cover.

elements.label: Temporal structure, prompt response, and signal quality
elements.desc: Benign activity has realistic temporal structure and signal quality: it clusters in business hours with realistic tails, and modeled users behave imperfectly (they click some phishing and report others, approve expected MFA prompts and decline unexpected ones, and dismiss some UAC or SmartScreen prompts). The diversity, entropy, and cross-user variance of this activity are measurable.

elements.label: Business-context lure plausibility
elements.desc: Decoys, deceptive credentials, decoy files, and honey-share content sit inside a plausible surrounding business context, so lures blend with genuine activity rather than standing out as obvious traps.

elements.label: Process and script execution telemetry
elements.desc: Process and script execution is recorded in detail, such as process creation with the full command line, parent process, hashes, and integrity level (Windows event 4688, Sysmon event 1), image and driver loads, PowerShell ScriptBlock, Module, and Transcript logging with deobfuscated content, AMSI verdicts, and auditd execve records on Linux.

elements.label: Authentication telemetry
elements.desc: Authentication produces realistic records across platforms. On Windows and Active Directory these include Kerberos events (4768, 4769, 4770, 4771), NTLM validation (4776), logon and logoff events (4624, 4625) with full logon-type and source fields, and account-management events (4720 through 4740); on Linux and Unix hosts they include PAM, sshd, and sudo entries in the authentication log and wtmp or btmp records. These records correlate across systems, so a single logon produces matching events on the client, the authenticating server (such as a domain controller), and the target.

elements.label: Object access: file, registry, and IPC
elements.desc: Access to files, the registry, and inter-process channels is auditable, such as file create, write, delete, and rename events (4663, Sysmon 11, 23, 26), registry set and delete events that record the before and after values (4657, Sysmon 12, 13, 14), named-pipe and raw-disk access events, and file-integrity-monitoring baselines on critical paths.

elements.label: Network telemetry
elements.desc: Network activity is observable at several layers, such as firewall or NGFW records with the 5-tuple, byte counts, duration, and verdict; Zeek, Suricata, or flow records carrying HTTP URI, host, and user-agent fields and TLS SNI and JA3 fields; DNS query and response logs with QNAME, QTYPE, and resolver; and NetFlow or IPFIX that supports volumetric analysis.

elements.label: Endpoint/EDR streams
elements.desc: Endpoint and EDR sensors emit deeper behavioral streams than the OS audit log alone. On Windows these include API-hook telemetry for sensitive calls (for example VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory), LSASS access attempts recorded with caller, target, and access mask, image loads annotated with signer and memory-loaded indicators, and memory-scan or YARA results. Equivalent sensors on Linux and macOS (such as eBPF-based agents or the EndpointSecurity framework) capture credential-access attempts, code injection, and suspicious memory activity in the same way.

elements.label: Identity-provider, cloud-plane, and application logs
elements.desc: Identity, cloud, and application layers produce their own logs, such as identity-provider sign-in logs with the conditional-access verdict and risk level; cloud audit logs (CloudTrail, Azure Activity, GCP Audit) recording principal, action, and resource; Kubernetes audit and admission logs; email tracking and mailbox audit; proxy or CASB logs with URL, user, bytes, and category; and application or database audit logs with session, user, and query. Many of these logs reside outside the enterprise-managed hosts, in the provider's control plane or SaaS tenant, and are retrieved through provider APIs rather than from the endpoints an attacker compromises, which gives them an independent vantage point.

elements.label: Schema fidelity, volume, and noise floor
elements.desc: The telemetry has realistic form and scale, not idealized labels. Events conform to real schemas (such as EVTX, Sysmon, Zeek, CloudTrail) with fields fully populated and in a typed, normalizable form (such as ECS or OCSF), volume reflects real fleet sizes, and the signal-to-noise ratio is realistic enough that naive rules produce realistic false positives. Crucially, events arise as a byproduct of activity rather than being injected as ground-truth answers.

elements.label: Coverage gaps, timing, and retention tiers
elements.desc: Telemetry coverage is uneven and imperfect, as it is in practice: some hosts run Sysmon and some do not, agents go offline and log shippers drop events, records carry both a generation and an ingestion timestamp with variable lag between them, and retention differs by source across hot, warm, and cold tiers.

elements.label: Integrity, enrichment, and cross-system correlation
elements.desc: Telemetry has integrity and analytic value beyond the endpoint. Clearing a local log does not remove the copy already in the SIEM, tampering with a sensor produces its own heartbeat-loss telemetry, and out-of-band collection survives endpoint compromise. Events are enriched with asset, user, geo, and threat-intelligence context under stable entity resolution, and carry joinable identifiers that support correlation across sources. Defenders can deploy sensors, tune their verbosity, and author and backtest detections against the collected data.
elements.anchors: T1070.001, T1685, T1588, UC-D5, UC-S1; D3-UBA, D3-MFA, D3-CE, D3-RA, D3-AMED

elements.label: Response outcome telemetry with explainable decisions
elements.desc: Defender responses produce analyzable, explainable records, not just state changes. Actions such as MFA challenges, credential changes, policy rollout, containment, hardening, eviction, mediation, tamper indicators, and restore validation are recorded with timestamps, actors, targets, outcomes, and provenance. Allow and deny decisions record the actor, resource, policy or rule, enforcement point, reason, and result, so a decision can be explained after the fact.
elements.anchors: D3-MFA, D3-PH, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED, D3-NTA, D3-UBA

elements.label: Recovery and deception telemetry
elements.desc: Recovery and deception both leave a telemetry trail. Recovery actions emit records covering source and provenance, integrity checks, the identity of the restored object, and post-recovery success or failure. Interactions with deceptions emit high-context records (such as first touch, attempted authentication, which decoy object was accessed, and any follow-on movement) that are retained for investigation.

elements.label: Command fidelity
elements.desc: Real shells (such as PowerShell, cmd, Bash, Zsh, or a Python REPL) are reachable as interactive or scripted sessions, and commands execute against the real operating system, returning real stdout and stderr and exit codes and honoring pipes, redirects, and subshells.

elements.label: API fidelity
elements.desc: Programmatic interfaces are reachable from tooling and behave as the real ones do, such as the Win32 and NT APIs, POSIX syscalls, cloud SDKs, and the Kubernetes API. Calls have real effects and return real error codes (for example ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED, EACCES, InvalidClientTokenId), and kernel-side telemetry fires on the call.

elements.label: Parameter space
elements.desc: Actions take real, typed, and often large parameter spaces, as exploits, lateral movement, and discovery queries do in reality. Wrong parameters produce wrong results rather than an automatic pass or fail, and tool help reflects the real options.

elements.label: Failure realism
elements.desc: Failure follows deterministically from a real mismatch rather than from a probability draw, and distinct failure modes (authentication, authorization, and reachability) are distinguishable. An agent can read the reason an action failed and adapt accordingly.

elements.label: Composition, tool transfer, and interactive sessions
elements.desc: Multi-step operations are composed by the agent from primitives rather than invoked as single super-actions. Tools are transferred over realistic channels (such as SMB, SCP, HTTP, or Invoke-WebRequest), each carrying a size, time, and telemetry cost, with in-memory alternatives available, and interactive sessions retain working directory, environment, and history.

elements.label: State effects, scheduling, and cost budgets
elements.desc: Actions that change state do so persistently and observably: side effects fire on registry, file-integrity, and process-creation telemetry, and destructive actions cannot be trivially reverted. Agents schedule work through real primitives that honor delays, and every action consumes time and generates telemetry, so there is a real cost budget.

elements.label: Observability, C2 semantics, and defender action
elements.desc: The agent sees the same output a human operator would, not a privileged behind-the-scenes view, and the output of a long-running command appears gradually as it runs rather than all at once at the end. When actions are issued over a command-and-control channel, they behave as they would over a real one: dispatch and results take time, that timing is irregular rather than constant, and commands can be queued or cancelled before they run. Defenders are held to the same standard, acting through the tools they would use operationally (for example querying a SIEM, isolating a host through EDR, pushing a firewall rule, or disabling an account) rather than through a single abstract respond action.
elements.anchors: T1071, T1104, UC-D2, UC-D4, UC-D5; D3-MFA, D3-CE, D3-PE, D3-RA, D3-RO, D3-NI, D3-AMED

elements.label: Privilege-scoped action surface and realistic tool-use idiom
elements.desc: The available action surface is scoped by privilege: the same command gives different output at different privilege levels, and privilege changes (through a token, sudo, or runas) alter what is possible in real time. The agent operates the full shell and API rather than a handcrafted menu, which lets evaluation use real tooling such as Metasploit, Impacket, BloodHound, or sqlmap.

elements.label: Distinct defender action families
elements.desc: The defender action space keeps distinct families separate rather than collapsing them into one generic respond, which would make defense unevaluable. These families include hardening (such as MFA, integrity controls, and allowlisting), credential operations (revoke, reset, disable, unlock, reissue), process eviction (suspend, terminate, kill a service, reboot, isolate), isolation and mediation (network isolation, host containment, session restriction, resource mediation), and restoration (file restore, reinstall, snapshot rollback, configuration rollback, integrity validation).
elements.anchors: D3-MFA, D3-PH, D3-AMED, D3-CE, D3-RA, D3-PE, D3-NI, D3-RO

elements.label: Multi-step defender operations require ordered sequencing
elements.desc: Multi-step defender operations proceed through ordered intermediate states rather than as one abstract super-action. Account recovery moves through review, then unlock, then reissue, then validation, then restored access; file restoration moves through source selection, then rollback, then integrity verification. Skipping these intermediate states would make the action surface unevaluable.

elements.label: Position- and privilege-dependent observability
elements.desc: What an agent can observe depends on where it sits and what privilege it holds. The traffic, hosts, and sessions it can see depend on its network position (a man-in-the-middle vantage is possible only where the topology allows it), the same query returns different detail at different privilege levels, administrative queries leave authentication trails, and pivoting to a new position changes what is observable.

elements.label: Partial, incremental, scale-appropriate results
elements.desc: Results arrive partial and at enterprise scale rather than as one complete answer. Enumerations paginate or throttle, large objects (such as user lists, BloodHound graphs, or cloud inventory) require many queries to assemble, and result volumes are large enough that an agent must parse and filter them rather than pattern-match a small fixed output.

elements.label: Noise, uncertainty, and time-variance
elements.desc: Observations carry noise and uncertainty and change over time. Scans return closed, filtered, or misleading responses, fingerprinting is probabilistic and banners can lie, stale entries can look live, and sessions end and caches expire. As a result, the agent cannot treat every value it receives as ground truth.

elements.label: Protocol, banner, and metadata fidelity on reception
elements.desc: Responses the agent receives conform to real protocols, such as SMB error codes, HTTP headers, and Kerberos error packets, with realistic timing and side channels. Host metadata is consistent across the different paths that reach it, and any inconsistency reflects a real condition or an attacker's manipulation rather than a simulator artifact.

elements.label: Defender-side observation surface
elements.desc: Defender agents observe events through a realistic interface, such as a SIEM search, an EDR console, or a SOAR queue, with real query syntax, pagination, and latency. Alerts carry timestamps, severity, source, asset, and supporting evidence, and there are two layers to work across (the alerts themselves and the raw events behind them) with the ability to pivot between them, so an analyst can move from an anomalous login to the related endpoint, service, and file activity in one workflow.

elements.label: Blind spots and observation cost
elements.desc: Both sides must reason about what they cannot see. An attacker cannot see behind a firewall or read another user's files without escalating first, and a defender cannot see what the sensors never captured. Every observation is itself an action that takes time and generates telemetry, so gaining visibility trades against speed and stealth.

elements.label: Feedback loops and multi-source correlation
elements.desc: Observation and action form a feedback loop: the output of one action informs the next observation and vice versa, which supports an iterative probe-and-adapt style of work. Both sides may also observe the same event from several sources that disagree, forcing them to reconcile across those sources.

elements.label: Oracle-free property
elements.desc: The agent is never handed ground-truth state directly. Everything it acts on must be derivable from observations it can actually make, with no leakage through the reward shape, the action mask, or the state vector. A defender reconstructs whether a host is compromised from alerts, sessions, traces, and decoy interactions rather than by reading a true status variable.

elements.label: Before/after response observation
elements.desc: Defender-facing views let a response outcome be compared against the state before it. After a quarantine, reachability, policy, and connection-failure events show whether containment actually took effect; after a restore, service-health and integrity checks show whether it succeeded. Without this before-and-after comparison, an action that merely fired cannot be told apart from one that worked.

elements.label: High-context deception observation
elements.desc: Interactions with decoys surface through a coherent investigation view rather than as scattered records: the first touch, the source identity, the decoy object involved, the sequence of actions, and any follow-on or lateral movement are linked together. A decoy dashboard or SIEM correlation makes the resulting chain navigable.

elements.label: Authoritative DNS, WHOIS, and certificate transparency
elements.desc: The external naming and certificate infrastructure resolves realistically, such as external DNS resolution with the full record set and reverse DNS, WHOIS and RDAP records with realistic registrants, passive-DNS history, certificate-transparency logs that reveal subdomains and staging hosts, and reachable certificate-revocation endpoints (OCSP and CRL).

elements.label: OSINT surface: public web, social, repos, paste
elements.desc: An open-source intelligence surface exists for reconnaissance, such as the target's public presence (website, job postings, filings), the social footprint of its employees, breach-dump corpora, code hosts (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) that contain accidentally committed secrets, paste sites (Pastebin, Gist), and search engines (Google, Shodan, Censys) that return realistic results.

elements.label: Threat intel, reputation, and external scanner observability
elements.desc: Threat-intelligence and reputation data is available to both sides: the reputation of a domain, IP address, or file hash can be queried, and the scores change as infrastructure ages. External scanners (such as Shodan, Censys, GreyNoise) index exposed services, so an attacker's reconnaissance is itself observable to the defender.

elements.label: Attacker-controlled domains, hosting, and C2 servers
elements.desc: An attacker can stand up realistic infrastructure: register domains (including typosquats and lookalike names that use similar-looking characters), and use rented, abuse-tolerant, or compromised hosting, each with a realistic cost and takedown latency. Command-and-control over HTTP(S), DNS, or a custom protocol is possible, with redirectors and multi-tier architectures, realistic TLS, and realistic domain categorization.

elements.label: Legitimate-service abuse for C2 blending
elements.desc: Legitimate services can be abused so that command-and-control blends into normal traffic, such as domain fronting through a CDN where it was historically possible, legitimate SaaS platforms (Dropbox, GitHub, Slack, Discord, Telegram) used as covert channels, and Tor or residential-proxy networks. Because these destinations are categorized as legitimate, naive detection does not flag them.

elements.label: Attacker email and weaponized content hosting
elements.desc: The attacker has email and content-hosting infrastructure, such as mail-sending infrastructure on attacker domains with configurable SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, phishing pages, fake login portals, and exploit-kit landing pages, and staged malware. Drive-by and watering-hole compromises are reachable from the simulated users.

elements.label: Compromised third parties and supply chain
elements.desc: Third parties and the supply chain can be compromised as attack paths, such as third-party websites, SaaS vendors, and update servers as compromisable entities, and vendor-to-MSP-to-target chains. Upstream package registries can be published to (through typosquatting, dependency confusion, or a compromised maintainer), and there are paths to compromise CI/CD pipelines.

elements.label: External identity and federation surface
elements.desc: The external identity and federation surface is modeled rather than collapsed to a single trusted domain. Vendor and federated identity providers carry realistic trust relationships, OAuth consent can be abused to grant access (illicit consent), external SAML and OIDC providers issue assertions that can be forged if their signing keys are compromised, and cross-tenant and cross-cloud boundaries are represented explicitly.

elements.label: Full DNS resolution path, infrastructure lifecycle, and cost/attribution
elements.desc: The full DNS resolution path can be traversed, from stub resolver to recursive resolver to root, TLD, and authoritative servers, which makes attacks such as DNS hijacking, domain-generation algorithms, and tunneling possible. Attacker infrastructure has a lifecycle (newly registered, aging, in use, then burned) with reputation propagating accordingly, and using it carries realistic monetary and time cost and leaves attribution trails.
